posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Greg’s pricing on “hot” items that haven’t been released yet and have no wine searcher hits are typically high. I think Kermit retail was about 15% lower to their select client list. Their retail tends to be 15-20% higher than regular pricing.

We’ll see what it ends up at. But one major retailer that buys a lot of allemand told me they passed on the initial offer they received at $200+ a btl. So I’m guessing it’ll be near that price opposed to 270+ for the reynard.

A decent amount of the stuff gets around.

I wouldn’t pay $200 for the chaillot in any lifetime. Reynard on the other hand O_O.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Glen Gold:

Yow. I picked up a couple of bottles of the 2012 for $150 last year (reluctantly, as I’d seen them at $129 and not pulled the trigger) and now… Yow. If the wine-searcher data is right, last summer his prices got driven up on pretty much all vintages. What happened?

2015 vintage anticipation. It’s not Allemand alone. Jamet is having the same issue as well as some of the other really hot/hip producers like Gonon.

posts.name: Thomas De Waen
posts.cooked: Bought some from a European retailer a couple of months back. They were offered at eur 125 for the Reynard and eur 110 for the chaillot (both prices are ex-VAT). That’s from a retailer that’s known to be making rather generous mark-ups. So there would have to be a lot of profit in the supply chain for it to retail at USD 250 in the US…

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
Thomas De Waen:

Bought some from a European retailer a couple of months back. They were offered at eur 125 for the Reynard and eur 110 for the chaillot (both prices are ex-VAT). That’s from a retailer that’s known to be making rather generous mark-ups. So there would have to be a lot of profit in the supply chain for it to retail at USD 250 in the US…

Yup, that’s the name of the game in the US for these wines. Even Jamet 2015 is close to USD200 here, as per Winesearcher. I haven’t bought since vintage 2010, but somehow I’m still in a European merchant’s email list and yesterday just emailed me their Jamet 2015 C-R prices at Euro89 and Euro190, for 750 and 1.5L respectively. I actually thought that these prices are still high and that there are other European merchants who would sell for less. I’ve blindly deleted some of their recent emails and not sure if they’ve gotten around to selling their Allemand yet.

posts.name: Frank Murray III
posts.cooked:
Sadly, I am out, too. I was able through the past years to buy and accumulate some bottles but at these prices, I’m done. I’d rather reallocate that money on champagne anyway, and at 51 years old, I am not enthusiastic anymore about buying new stuff and then seeing wisdom in waiting 10, 15 years for it to mature.

If I manage to find some over in Europe this year when I am there, I will drag some home with me.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I’m out.

Wonderful wines, but there are lots of high quality Northern Rhone wines at sub-$100. Heck, I paid sub-$50 for Gonon, sub-$75 for Juge, around $65 for Levet and I think around $75 for Benetiere. Lots of other very strong 2015s for far far less.

Heck, I can get the stunning '98 Jamet for less than that.

What is the price of the Sans Soufre?

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
J. Galang:

Is this really going to be the going prices for 2015’s?

Chaillot - $250
Reynard - $270

No way. Those prices would require the wholesale price to double from 2014 to 2015. That hasn’t/won’t happen. I haven’t received my allocation from Kermit, but if I had to guess, I’d say $150/$175. Hell, at those prices, Alfie can fill his “water” bottles with it.

posts.cooked: Somehow I would suspect that even if priced obscenely by Berserkers standards, i.e. even with the chorus of “I’m out” here, there are still those who would buy such that the market will bear the so-called absurd pricing. Evidenced by the current rage in select and quite obscure Northern Rhone wines these days.

Absurd pricing. Great wine I’m sure, for the rich. So much good No. Rhône out there for so much less.

I would be very pleased if wholesalers and retailers get stuck with their allocations - serves them right for taking advantage of consumers, though they have every right to do it

I’ve been really impressed by the more classic producers and their takes on 15.

I have not had the 2015 flagship cuvee, but was not that impressed with the 2015 Gilles Les Rieux and put an “avoid” on its 2015 gamay:

WineBerserkers – 25 Jan 18

TN: 2015 Guillaume Gilles Ardèche Les Massardieres - Avoid

Really disappointed with this wine. I have generally come to love gamay from Northern Rhone. Souhaut makes an excellent one. So when I see that the new Cornas hot-shot has released one as well, I jumped. Only $25, so a solid QPR if it is the...

Did like the 2012 Cornas:

WineBerserkers – 7 Dec 17

TN: 2012 Guillaume Gilles Cornas

Yea another baby, but never having had this wine, wanted to check in on the handful that I grabbed on the Crush sale at a very decent price. I note some folks here have recently been talking this wine up. Less open-knit than many other 2012...

Will grab some 2015s as well.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Surely Thierry’s impending retirement must be a factor here too?

William Kelley:

Surely Thierry’s impending retirement must be a factor here too?

I’m sure it does, but probably less so than the 2015 hype and the seemingly recent discovery of Cote Rôtie, Cornas and St J by more mainstream wine collectors as Burgundy prices have gone stratospheric. The same thing has happened with Piemonte.

While I may scoff at people spending thousands of dollars for old bottles of Gentaz and Trollat, there are lots of folks getting in line at the unicorn market.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
NoahR:
William Kelley:

Surely Thierry’s impending retirement must be a factor here too?

I’m sure it does, but probably less so than the 2015 hype and the seemingly recent discovery of Cote Rôtie, Cornas and St J by more mainstream wine collectors as Burgundy prices have gone stratospheric. The same thing has happened with Piemonte.

While I may scoff at people spending thousands of dollars for old bottles of Gentaz and Trollat, there are lots of folks getting in line at the unicorn market.

I guess one scoffs because they were so cheap, so recently. But I have tasted plenty of wines even more expensive than the 1990 Gentaz I drank Saturday night, and few that were as good.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:
Charles Zedlewski:
asnitow:

On the topic of rhone prices, if you hurry to winebid right now you too can buy bottles of 2014 Juge for $430 each - before buyers premium, of course.

The Allemand price increases are disappointing, but Juge has to be the single most overrated northern rhone today.

At real release prices, I’d say it’s the best value in Northern Rhône.

I’d say that for 90%+ of all wines I bought at release. But not all of them proved to be the best value.

William Kelley:
NoahR:
William Kelley:

Surely Thierry’s impending retirement must be a factor here too?

While I may scoff at people spending thousands of dollars for old bottles of Gentaz and Trollat, there are lots of folks getting in line at the unicorn market.

I guess one scoffs because they were so cheap, so recently. But I have tasted plenty of wines even more expensive than the 1990 Gentaz I drank Saturday night, and few that were as good.

I only learned about Gentaz and Trollat and the old guard of the Northern Rhône a few years ago. They were already unicorns by then. Do they merit being priced twice as high as similar vintages of, say, Chave hermitage or La Chapelle? Dunno - I can’t afford any of them.

I certainly can’t afford Allemand at $250 a pop. The people who buy them will always claim they are worth it, and the people priced out will always say the markup is absurd and there are so many good wines for so much less.

I’m still annoyed at how expensive Gonon, Levet and even Gilles Barge have become.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked: Interesting, Charlie, about Greg’s requirement, and also that (apparently) plenty of folks where quite ready and willing to bite on Reynard at that price. I’ve not had the privilege of having a Reynard yet, but I do love the Chaillot. I’m not sure I could definitely say I don’t think it’s “worth it,” but I can definitely say I’m not interested in paying that tariff! But, mostly, that’s borne out of a function whereby I almost never spend that much on any wine … plus, there’s just so many other really nice Syrahs that cost less. A price jump this dramatic sure does feel like a bucket of cold water, though. It will be interesting to see who will take that bath.
